en.extract:
The Papuan frogmouth (Podargus papuensis) is a species of bird in the family Podargidae, found in the Aru Islands, New Guinea, and in Cape York Peninsula, Queensland, Australia.


Taxonomy
The species was originally described by zoologist Jean René Constant Quoy and naturalist Joseph Paul Gaimard in 1830.
The three subspecies are P. p. papuensis, P. p. baileyi, and P. p. rogersi.


Description

The Papuan frogmouth is the largest of frogmouths in terms of length. Average sizes indicate that it only falls behind the Neotropical great potoo and oilbird (if the latter is a true member of the order) among the largest species in the order Caprimulgiformes. On average these birds are about 53 cm (21 in), with a range of 50 to 60 cm (20 to 24 in). This species was found to average 414 g (14.6 oz) in males and 314 g (11.1 oz) in females, with a total range of 290 to 570 g (10 to 20 oz). The tawny frogmouth is smaller on average than this but is capable of reaching higher maximum weights. The Papuan frogmouth has a bulbous bill, red eye, cream eyebrow, long tail and dark wings. The male of the species is slightly larger, darker and marbled in appearance. The female is more rufous in appearance.
P. p. baileyi is smaller, and darker.  P. p. rogersi is larger and paler.
Similar species include the tawny frogmouth. The Papuan frogmouth is larger, has red eyes, a longer tail, and darker wings.


Distribution and habitat
It is found in the Aru Islands, New Guinea, and Cape York Peninsula.
This species' natural habitat is subtropical or tropical, moist, lowland forests.


Behaviour


Call
The call is a resonant 'ooom' or a laughing hoot. It is usually heard after dusk and before dawn.


Breeding
Breeding takes place from August to January. One or two white eggs are placed in a nest consisting of a few sticks placed in the fork of a branch.


Feeding
The Papuan frogmouth is strictly nocturnal. It hunts for large insects on the ground from dusk. On occasion, it also takes small reptiles, amphibians, or birds as prey.
The Papuan frogmouth may secrete a substance in its mouth that attracts flies. According to a number of observers, it is able to wait with its mouth open and flies enter to investigate the odor.


References


External links

 Media related to Podargus papuensis at Wikimedia Commons

The '''Papuan frogmouth''' ('''''Podargus papuensis''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Podargidae]], found in the [[Aru Islands]], [[New Guinea]], and in [[Cape York Peninsula]], [[Queensland]], Australia.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Species profile—Podargus papuensis (Papuan frogmouth) |url=https://apps.des.qld.gov.au/species-search/details/?id=1953 |access-date=2024-04-04 |website=Species information |publisher=Queensland Government}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
The species was originally described by zoologist [[Jean René Constant Quoy]] and naturalist [[Joseph Paul Gaimard]] in 1830.

The three subspecies are ''P. p. papuensis'', ''P. p. baileyi'', and ''P. p. rogersi''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Papuan Frogmouth|url=http://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=BA1D64E2E70D8E4D|website=Avibase |access-date=7 January 2015}}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:Podargus papuensis -Papuan Frogmouth-8.jpg|thumb|upright|Papuan frogmouth in Papua New Guinea]]

[[File:Papuanfrogmouth-night-2024.jpg|thumb|left|180px|Papuan frogmouth, [[Julatten, Queensland]]]]

The Papuan frogmouth is the largest of [[frogmouth]]s in terms of length. Average sizes indicate that it only falls behind the [[Neotropic]]al [[great potoo]] and [[oilbird]] (if the latter is a true member of the order) among the largest species in the order [[Caprimulgiformes]].{{citation needed|date=January 2015}} On average these birds are about {{convert|53|cm|in|abbr=on}}, with a range of {{convert|50|to|60|cm|in|abbr=on}}. This species was found to average {{convert|414|g|oz|abbr=on}} in males and {{convert|314|g|oz|abbr=on}} in females, with a total range of {{convert|290|to|570|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name=CRC>{{cite book |title=CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses |edition=2nd |editor-first=John B. |editor-last=Dunning Jr. |publisher=CRC Press |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-4200-6444-5}}</ref> The [[tawny frogmouth]] is smaller on average than this but is capable of reaching higher maximum weights. The Papuan frogmouth has a bulbous bill, red eye, cream eyebrow, long tail and dark wings. The male of the species is slightly larger, darker and marbled in appearance. The female is more rufous in appearance.

''P. p. baileyi'' is smaller, and darker.  ''P. p. rogersi'' is larger and paler.<ref name="pizzey" />

Similar species include the [[tawny frogmouth]]. The Papuan frogmouth is larger, has red eyes, a longer tail, and darker wings.<ref name="pizzey" />

==Distribution and habitat==
It is found in the [[Aru Islands]], [[New Guinea]], and [[Cape York Peninsula]].

This species' natural [[habitat]] is [[subtropical]] or [[tropical]], moist, lowland [[forest]]s.

==Behaviour==

===Call===
The call is a resonant 'ooom' or a laughing hoot. It is usually heard after dusk and before dawn.<ref name=Strange/>

===Breeding===
Breeding takes place from August to January.<ref name="pizzey">{{Cite book|title=Field Guide to the Birds of Australia |last=Pizzey |first=Graham |author2=Knight, Frank |year=1997 |publisher=[[HarperCollinsPublishers]] |location=Sydney, Australia |isbn=0-207-18013-X |page=308}}</ref> One or two white eggs are placed in a nest consisting of a few sticks placed in the fork of a branch.<ref name="pizzey" />

===Feeding===
The Papuan frogmouth is strictly nocturnal. It hunts for large [[insect]]s on the ground from dusk.<ref name=Strange>{{cite book |last=Strange |first=Morten |title=A Photographic Guide to the Birds of Indonesia |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0hnRAgAAQBAJ&pg=PT592 |edition=2nd |year=2013 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-1-4629-1032-8 |page=592}}</ref> On occasion, it also takes small reptiles, amphibians, or birds as prey.<ref name="fb">{{cite web|last1=Ekins|first1=Graham|title=Papuan Frogmouth eating a Willie Wagtail|url=https://www.facebook.com/graham.ekins/posts/1581329661896536|website=Facebook|access-date=8 March 2017}}</ref>

The Papuan frogmouth may secrete a substance in its mouth that attracts flies.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Diamond |first=Jared |author-link=Jared Diamond |title=Stinking Birds and Burning Books |journal=Natural History |url=https://archive.org/stream/naturalhistory10301amer/naturalhistory10301amer_djvu.txt |volume=103 |issue=2 |date=February 1994 |access-date=7 January 2015}} Online at the [[Internet Archive]].</ref> According to a number of observers, it is able to wait with its mouth open and flies enter to investigate the odor.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Corliss|first=William|title=Flies fly into frogmouth's mouth |journal=Science Frontiers |date=March–April 1994|issue=92|url=http://www.science-frontiers.com/sf092/sf092b05.htm |access-date=7 January 2015}}</ref>
